title: SEGMENT 10 — Reading the fingerprints: cancer genomics

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Reading the fingerprints: cancer genomics
Welcome back.
Now I want to tell you about something that has fundamentally changed how we understand cancer in the last twenty years: the ability to read a tumor's complete genome.
Cheaply, fast, and at scale.
Until roughly the year 2000, classifying cancer was done by a pathologist looking at a slice of tumor under a microscope.
You'd say things like "this is a grade three invasive ductal carcinoma of the breast.
" Useful, but coarse.
It told you what the cells looked like, not what was wrong with them genetically.

Then the human genome project finished, sequencing got dramatically cheaper, and starting around 2005 a massive international project called The Cancer Genome Atlas — TCGA, no relation to the DNA bases — started sequencing thousands of tumors across dozens of cancer types.
The goal was to ask, for every common cancer: what mutations does it actually have?
At the level of the genome, what is breast cancer?
What is colon cancer?
Are they one disease each, or many?
The answer was: many.
Breast cancer, looked at genomically, is at least four or five different diseases that happen to occur in the same organ.
Lung cancer is similar.
Colon cancer is similar.
The morphology under the microscope was hiding a much richer molecular taxonomy underneath.
A useful concept here: driver mutations versus passenger mutations.
A typical adult cancer carries thousands of mutations — most of them random damage that happened to the cell on its way to becoming cancer but didn't contribute to the cancerous behavior.
Those are passengers — along for the ride.
A small handful, usually four to ten, are drivers — mutations that actually conferred a fitness advantage to the cell.
Telling drivers from passengers is one of the harder problems in cancer genomics, and it's done by looking across many tumors and asking which mutations recur more often than chance would predict.
Now here's a beautiful idea that came out of cancer genomics.
Different things that cause mutations leave different patterns.
The fingerprint analogy is perfect here.
Imagine you find a smashed window.
You can dust the windowsill for fingerprints.
Different criminals leave different prints.

UV light damages DNA in a very specific way — it tends to fuse adjacent cytosines, and when the cell tries to fix this, you get a characteristic pattern of C-to-T substitutions, especially at certain sequence contexts.
Tobacco smoke contains chemicals that bind to a specific base, guanine, and leave a different pattern — predominantly G-to-T substitutions.
Aging itself, just the slow background hum of metabolic damage, leaves another pattern.
Certain DNA repair defects leave their own characteristic patterns.
A cellular enzyme called APOBEC, which normally edits viral DNA but sometimes turns on the cell's own genome, leaves yet another.
By analyzing the entire spectrum of mutations in a tumor, we can read its history.
We can tell whether a lung cancer was caused by smoking or arose in a non-smoker.
We can tell whether a skin cancer was driven by UV exposure.
We can tell whether a tumor has a particular DNA-repair defect — and that has direct therapeutic implications.
The most therapeutically important fingerprint is mismatch repair deficiency.
Some tumors lose the cellular machinery that proofreads new DNA, and they end up with thousands of small mutations, especially at repetitive sequences called microsatellites.
We call these tumors microsatellite instability high, or MSI-high.
They have an enormously high tumor mutation burden — TMB-high.
Why does this matter?
Because every mutation in a protein-coding region is a chance to produce a slightly weird protein fragment that the immune system might recognize as foreign.
MSI-high tumors are like a fortress with a thousand cracks in the walls — the immune system has many places to peek in.
And it turns out that MSI-high tumors respond beautifully to immunotherapy, regardless of where in the body they arose.
This led to the first FDA approval of a cancer drug based on a molecular feature rather than the organ of origin — a tumor-agnostic approval.
Genomics turned cancer classification on its head.
Cancer genomics also drives precision medicine.
When a patient is diagnosed with lung cancer today, in many centers their tumor is sequenced for a panel of known driver mutations.
The result tells the oncologist which drug to use.
EGFR mutation?
Use an EGFR inhibitor.
ALK rearrangement?
Use an ALK inhibitor.
KRAS G12C?
Use a KRAS G12C inhibitor.
The genome of the tumor has become a prescription.
